title: Cutting a halving joint
subtitle: Mark and cut a basic cross or T halving joint by hand
excerpt: How to cut a basic halving joint in softwood by hand: marking out, cutting the cheek and shoulder, and chiselling the waste. Covers cross-halving, T-halving, and corner-halving in the same pass.
sourceNotes: Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Carpentry for Boys (1914).

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple joint where each piece is cut back to half its thickness so the two pieces meet flush at the cross or T. Used for light framing, shelf supports, and basic carcass work where the load is shared rather than pulled.
A cut made square across the face of the timber, defining the end of the joint. The shoulder controls the visible joint line and must be cut on the waste side of the line.

content.text: A
halving joint

is the first hand-cut joint a carpenter learns. Each piece loses half its thickness so the two pieces sit flush. Cross-halving in the middle, T-halving at a side, corner-halving at the end; all three follow the same pass.
When to use this technique

Use the halving joint for light frame work: a garden gate brace, a tool-shed wall plate to stud, a simple shelf rail crossing a back batten. The joint is fine for compression loads. For tension or twisting loads, choose a mortise and tenon instead.
What you need

Two pieces of timber the same thickness. A tenon saw with 13 to 15 teeth per inch. A bench chisel matching the width of the joint or wider. A combination square for marking out. A pencil and a marking knife if available; a sharp pencil works for a beginner.
Step 1, mark the width on both pieces

Lay the two pieces side by side. Mark the width of the joint on the face of each piece: the width is the thickness of the crossing piece. Square the lines across each face with the combination square. Carry the lines down both edges so the cuts can be tracked from any side.
Step 2, mark the depth on the edges

Set the marking gauge or combination square rule to half the thickness of the timber. Mark a depth line along each edge between the two width lines. This depth line is the floor of the cut for each piece.
Step 3, cut the shoulder cuts
Hold the timber on the bench with the marking line vertical. Saw down the width line on the waste side of the mark, stopping at the depth line. The cuts are called
shoulder cuts
. Repeat at the other width line. The saw cuts must be square; check by holding the square against the saw blade in line with the cut.
Step 4, remove the waste

Lay the timber flat on the bench. Place the bench chisel on the cut surface at one shoulder. Tap with the hammer to lift a chip from the waste. Work across to the other shoulder in small chip-and-lift steps. Do not try to take the whole waste out in one cut; the chisel pushes through the depth line and the joint sits proud.
Step 5, pare the floor flat

Hold the chisel flat on the bottom of the cut, bevel up. Push by hand, no hammer. Pare the floor flat to the depth line. The floor must be true, not rounded; the crossing piece sits on the whole floor and a high spot tips the joint.
Step 6, fit and check

Place the crossing piece into the cut. The two pieces should sit flush, with no gap at the shoulders. If the joint is tight, take a thin shaving from each side wall, not the floor. If the joint is loose, the marks were not on the waste side. Glue with PVA and clamp or screw through the face. A halving joint glued and screwed holds for the life of a softwood frame.
What it looks like when it is right
The two pieces sit at right angles to each other, flush on every face. No gap shows at the shoulder lines. The joint holds steady under a hand push.
Common mistakes
Cutting on the wrong side of the marking line. The joint comes out wide by the saw kerf and shows a gap at the shoulder. Always cut on the waste side and pare back to the line if needed.
Chiselling the waste out in one chip. The chisel splits past the depth line into the side wall and the joint sits proud. Take the waste in small steps from one shoulder across to the other.
Rounded floor. A rounded floor tips the crossing piece off square. Pare the floor flat by holding the chisel flat on the bench, bevel up.
